articles.title: Law Firm Taft Inks 17K SF at Durst’s 1155 Avenue of the Americas for First NYC Office
articles.narrative: National law firm Taft has secured its first New York City office, leasing 16,563 square feet on the entire 24th floor of The Durst Organization's 1155 Avenue of the Americas. The firm, specializing in employment law and corporate transactions, chose the Midtown tower for its strategic location near Bryant Park and major transit hubs, aiming to establish a strong foundation for nationwide growth. The new office will house an energy, infrastructure, and project finance group, marking a significant expansion for Taft following recent mergers that boosted its attorney count and revenue.

data_points.address: 1155 Avenue of the Americas, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: 42-story tower located along the western blockfront of Avenue of the Americas between West 44th and West 45th streets, two blocks from Bryant Park and the New York Public Library.

articles.market_intelligence: Midtown's office asking rents averaged $84.42 per square foot in April.

articles.title: SL Green Sells 49% Stake in 346 Madison Avenue to Mori Building Co.
articles.narrative: SL Green Realty has sold a 49% stake in 346 Madison Avenue to Mori Building Company, valuing the office tower at $175 million. The two companies will form a joint venture to develop a new 46-story, 850,000-square-foot office tower at the Midtown site. SL Green will retain a 51% stake and manage the development and leasing, aiming to meet strong tenant demand for high-quality, well-located office space in East Midtown.

data_points.address: 346 Madison Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: New 46-story tower to feature a two-floor amenity center with an auditorium, tenant lounge, wellness center, outdoor terrace, and fitness center, plus ground-floor retail.
data_points.project_notes: SL Green sold a 49% stake in the existing property to form a joint venture for a new development.

articles.title: Bridge City Capital Provides $21M Loan for Greenpoint Dev Site
articles.narrative: Bridge City Capital has provided a $21 million acquisition and construction loan to Rogers Builders & Development for the purchase of a parking lot in Greenpoint, Brooklyn. The developer plans to construct a 99-unit mixed-use multifamily project on the site, taking advantage of the 485x tax abatement program. The financing package, with a 12-month term and extension options, represents an 80 percent loan-to-value ratio, reflecting a growing trend in construction financing where developers face challenges in securing equity.

data_points.notable_features: Site includes an obsolete water tower and offers 54,375 buildable square feet as-of-right, or 70,687 square feet with Universal Affordability Preference program.
data_points.project_notes: The 74-foot by 200-foot parcel was recently subdivided by the seller. The planned 99-unit project is designed to skirt the 485x tax abatement program's wage requirement for new construction.

articles.title: Barnes & Noble Inks 11K-SF Retail Lease for New East Village Store
articles.narrative: Barnes & Noble has signed an 11,300-square-foot retail lease for a new store in Manhattan's East Village, located at the base of the Steiner East Village condominium building. The new store, which is expected to open in November, marks a significant expansion for the bookstore chain, which plans to open 60 new locations across the U.S. in 2026.

data_points.address: 181 Avenue A, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Located at the base of an 82-unit condominium building with amenities including a residents lounge, fitness center, swimming pool, and landscaped rooftop terrace.

articles.market_intelligence: Average asking rent for retail space in the nearby area of 14th to 23rd streets was $320 per square foot for the first quarter of 2026.

articles.title: Topeka’s Housing Market Builds Momentum as Demand Outpaces Supply
articles.narrative: Topeka, Kansas, is experiencing a highly competitive and resilient housing market, driven by strong demand and undersupply. The market is characterized by rapid home sales, with properties selling at or above list price, and a median home price significantly lower than the national average. This demand is fueled by job opportunities, affordability, and regional connectivity, attracting developers to address the housing shortage. Key projects underway include The Annex Group's 250-unit affordable housing development, Union at Tower District, and Flaherty & Collins Properties' 192-unit market-rate apartment community, The Hutch, both aiming to diversify the city's housing stock.

articles.title: IPA Arranges $29M in Construction Financing for Northwest Houston Industrial Project
articles.narrative: Institutional Property Advisors (IPA) has secured $29 million in construction financing for Lifestyle Industrial, an 84-unit industrial condominium project in northwest Houston. The development, undertaken by X Space Group, is designed to integrate office, retail, and entertainment elements. The financing will support the construction of these condo-style industrial units.

data_points.notable_features: Condo-style industrial units designed to incorporate elements of office, retail, and entertainment.

articles.title: RealSource Arranges $5.4M Sale of Single-Tenant Retail Building in Kemah, Texas
articles.narrative: RealSource Group has facilitated the $5.4 million sale of a single-tenant retail building in Kemah, Texas, which is net leased to Mister Car Wash. The property, constructed in 2021, features a fully guaranteed corporate lease. The transaction involved a local private investor as the seller and an undisclosed buyer.

data_points.notable_features: Single-tenant building net leased to Mister Car Wash with a fully guaranteed corporate lease.

articles.title: Smurfit Westrock Signs 78,683 SF Industrial Lease in Northwest Houston
articles.narrative: Smurfit Westrock, a packaging services provider, has signed a 78,683-square-foot industrial lease in northwest Houston. The company will occupy space within Patriot Business Park, a three-building industrial development totaling 462,591 square feet. The transaction highlights continued demand for industrial space in the Houston market.

data_points.notable_features: Three-building industrial development totaling 462,591 square feet.

articles.title: CanTex Capital Buys Four Industrial Outdoor Storage Facilities Totaling 47,465 SF in Dallas
articles.narrative: CanTex Capital has acquired four industrial outdoor storage (IOS) facilities totaling 47,465 square feet in Dallas's Brookhollow neighborhood. The investment firm plans to undertake capital improvements on three of the four sites, including renovations to office and warehouse spaces and functional yard enhancements. The seller and sale price were not disclosed.

articles.title: Fading momentum? Not in Detroit, according to the Detroit Commercial Real Estate Mid-Year Forecast
articles.narrative: The Detroit Commercial Real Estate Mid-Year Forecast, hosted by Michigan Real Estate Journal, highlighted continued momentum across the Detroit-area CRE market. Industrial, retail, multifamily, and healthcare sectors demonstrated solid performance, with a cautiously optimistic outlook for the office sector despite ongoing challenges. The forecast also emphasized significant development and construction activity in downtown Detroit and its suburbs, contributing to a positive future for the region's commercial real estate professionals. Demand for apartments in both the city and its surrounding communities continues to rise, underscoring the strength of the multifamily market.

people.name: Jack Johns
people.title: First Vice President, Institutional Properties, Multifamily, Capital Markets

people.name: Steven Silverman
people.title: Senior Vice President, National Investment & Brokerage Advisory, Auction Services

articles.title: Lee & Associates closes 26,707-square-foot industrial lease in Fort Worth
articles.narrative: Lee & Associates Dallas-Fort Worth has successfully brokered a new lease for a 26,707-square-foot industrial facility in Fort Worth, Texas. The transaction involved Hawk Logistics as the tenant, securing space at Riverbend East Building 1. Brokers from Lee & Associates represented the tenant, while a team from CBRE Inc. and HPI Real Estate Services & Investments represented the landlord.

companies_people.label: LEASING AGENT
companies_people.firm_name: HPI Real Estate Services & Investments – Dallas

articles.title: General manager with Chicago’s Draper and Kramer wins property manager award
articles.narrative: Kelli Stuart, a general manager and assistant vice president at Draper and Kramer, Incorporated, received the 2026 Certified Property Manager (CPM) Excellence Award from the Institute of Real Estate Management (IREM)'s Chicago chapter. The award recognizes her excellence in real estate management, leadership, community service, and engagement with IREM. Stuart, who has 17 years of industry experience, currently manages three high-profile residential properties in Chicago's Gold Coast neighborhood, demonstrating versatility in delivering exceptional residential experiences and asset performance.

data_points.notable_features: Kelli Stuart manages 1350-1360 Lake Shore Drive, 61 Banks Street, and 1420 Lake Shore Drive in Chicago's Gold Coast neighborhood.

people.name: Kelli Stuart
people.title: General Manager and Assistant Vice President

people.name: Julie Stevlingson
people.title: Senior Vice President and Director of Management Services

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: Institute of Real Estate Management (IREM) Chicago Chapter

articles.title: Essex Realty Group closes sale of six-unit multifamily property in Chicago’s Lincoln Park neighborhood
articles.narrative: Essex Realty Group has facilitated the sale of a six-unit multifamily property located at 1948 North Lincoln Avenue in Chicago's Lincoln Park neighborhood. The property, featuring one-bedroom, one-bathroom apartments with modern finishes and in-unit amenities, offers investors strong in-place rents and significant upside potential through continued rental growth and potential short-term rental income. Its prime location provides excellent access to transportation, dining, shopping, and entertainment, benefiting from proximity to major attractions and DePaul University, which drives consistent rental demand.

data_points.notable_features: Each apartment features modern finishes including hardwood floors, stainless steel appliances, granite countertops, tiled backsplashes and bathrooms, individual HVAC systems, in-unit laundry, and private rear decks. Located less than one mile from Sedgwick and Armitage CTA Stations, with convenient access to Lake Shore Drive and multiple bus routes.

articles.market_intelligence: Lincoln Park remains one of Chicago’s most historically significant and desirable neighborhoods due to its proximity to downtown Chicago, Lake Michigan, and the city’s lakefront amenities. Its combination of historic charm, vibrant retail corridors, and steady residential demand continues to make the neighborhood a premier destination for multifamily investment. DePaul University, with over 25,000 students, continues to drive strong rental demand throughout the neighborhood.

articles.title: Gerdom Realty and Investment closes lease for hobby shop in White Lake retail center
articles.narrative: Gerdom Realty and Investment successfully brokered a lease for a 1,625-square-foot retail space at White Lake Plaza in White Lake, Michigan, to a hobby shop. This transaction brings the retail center to full occupancy.

articles.title: Bill Koch’s Aspen estate heads to auction asking $99M
articles.narrative: Billionaire industrialist Bill Koch is sending his 52-acre Elk Mountain Lodge compound in Aspen, CO, to an online auction with a current asking price of $99 million. The property, which includes a nearly 17,000-square-foot main lodge and seven guest cabins, was previously listed for $125 million. The move to auction is intended to cast a wider net for ultra-high net worth buyers and determine the property's true market value, given the limited buyer pool for such high-value listings.

data_points.notable_features: The compound includes a main lodge with eight bedrooms and 10 bathrooms, seven guest cabins, a fitness center, two hot tubs, and two ponds.
data_points.project_notes: Bill Koch originally acquired the property for $26.5 million in 2007. He previously attempted to sell the property for $100 million over 10 years ago, and later for $80 million in 2016. A 31-acre portion of the original property was sold for $14.5 million in 2020. The remaining 52 acres were listed for rent at $35,000 per night in 2022.

articles.market_intelligence: The Aspen luxury market has experienced significant growth, with sales volume increasing by nearly 40% last year to over $2.5 billion. Median sale prices in the area rose 13% from 2025 to over $5.5 million. Restrictive zoning measures have limited new supply, contributing to the market's strength. A recent record sale in 2024 saw a mansion trade for $108 million, and the market currently features the priciest listing in America at $300 million.

articles.title: Centerbridge eyeing minority stake in industrial firm
articles.narrative: Centerbridge Partners is reportedly in advanced discussions to acquire a minority stake in Merritt Properties, a Maryland-based industrial and office firm with a portfolio exceeding 20 million square feet. The private equity group is expected to purchase approximately one-third of the business from Almanac Realty Investors, the real estate investment division of Neuberger Berman. The Merritt family will retain a majority ownership in the company they founded in the 1960s. This potential acquisition highlights Centerbridge's growing investment in the real estate sector, following the close of its $2.3 billion second real estate fund in early 2022.

data_points.notable_features: Portfolio includes warehouses, distribution centers, and office buildings across Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, and Florida.

articles.title: Housing Notes: Land appreciates, homes depreciate
articles.narrative: This opinion column argues that the appreciation in residential real estate value primarily stems from the land, not the physical structure. The author contends that a home's value, excluding the land, depreciates over time unless there is continuous investment in maintenance and renovations. Conversely, land values tend to rise over the long term in most housing markets, offsetting the structural depreciation. The author emphasizes that homeownership requires constant investment in the house to keep pace with market expectations, as neighbors are typically doing the same. The piece concludes by suggesting that understanding land's behavior as an asset class is crucial for addressing affordable housing challenges.

articles.title: Construction costs pile onto housing crunch as copper, lumber spike
articles.narrative: This article analyzes how surging construction material costs, including copper, lumber, diesel, and aluminum, are exacerbating the housing affordability crisis. Geopolitical events, tariffs, and supply chain disruptions are driving up expenses for homebuilders, leading to increased uncertainty and fewer options for affordable homeownership. The National Association of Home Builders reports that material cost volatility is making it difficult for 70% of builders to price homes, with executives from major homebuilders anticipating higher prices later in the year.

articles.title: PGIM bets big on homebuilder land banks with $4B deployment
articles.narrative: PGIM, the asset management arm of Prudential Financial, has deployed approximately $4 billion into residential land-bank deals through a partnership with Domain Real Estate Partners. This strategy involves homebuilders selling undeveloped lots to a financing partner and then repurchasing them as construction progresses, allowing builders to maintain liquidity and a future housing pipeline without tying up capital in land holdings. The move reflects a broader institutional investor trend towards asset-based finance, providing exposure to long-term housing demand while mitigating direct development risks.

data_points.project_notes: PGIM and Domain Real Estate Partners launched their partnership last year and have completed seven residential land-bank transactions.

articles.title: NYC’s top deals: Tribeca parking garage sells — finally — for $59M
articles.narrative: Alchemy Properties has acquired a former parking garage at 56 North Moore Street in Tribeca for $58.5 million from the Calicchio family, who had owned the property for decades. The property was previously marketed as a potential office conversion opportunity. Additionally, an LLC tied to Rafael Rabinowitz purchased a five-story, 29,000-square-foot office building at 267 Broadway in Tribeca for $30 million from a company linked to the Roe Corporation. In the Flatiron District, a 10-story multifamily property with eight apartments at 16 East 18th Street, spanning nearly 20,300 square feet, was sold for $16.3 million by an LLC tied to Revere Capital to an LLC linked to Eun Rae Jo.

data_points.address: 56 North Moore Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Former parking garage, previously marketed for office conversion.
data_points.project_notes: The Calicchio family had owned the building for decades.

companies_people.label: BUYER
companies_people.firm_name: LLC linked to Eun Rae Jo

articles.market_intelligence: Ground-up construction in NYC is becoming harder to finance, leading builders to focus on redevelopment projects. Manhattan is seeing large office-to-residential conversions, while Brooklyn's redevelopment involves smaller expansions of residential homes in low-rise neighborhoods.

articles.title: PolicyPro: SEQRA reform goes live, pied-à-terre tax set for July start
articles.narrative: New York State has enacted significant reforms to the State Environmental Quality Review Act (SEQRA), streamlining the approval process for various housing projects. The changes, effective immediately, exempt projects up to 500 units in high-density areas of New York City and smaller developments in other urban and rural communities from lengthy environmental reviews, a move praised by developers and housing advocates for reducing costs and delays. Concurrently, a new pied-à-terre tax is set to take effect on July 1, targeting one- to three-family homes valued at $5 million or more, and condos/co-ops assessed at $1 million or more, with phased-in rates. This tax, which includes exemptions for primary residences and full-time rentals, is designed to sunset in five years, while a proposed cash transfer tax was dropped.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: New York Solar Energy Industries Association

articles.title: Mamdani reveals his “bad landlord” playbook
articles.narrative: Mayor Zohran Mamdani's administration has unveiled a comprehensive 112-page housing plan, "Block by Block," aimed at removing "bad actor" landlords from the market. The strategy involves collaborating with tenant organizations to identify target buildings, conducting "roof-to-cellar" inspections to find violations, and pursuing criminal charges against property owners. The plan also outlines methods to remove existing owners, including engaging with lenders to initiate foreclosures and aggressively utilizing the 7A program to appoint HPD-monitored administrators. Additionally, the administration intends to support mission-driven owners and community land trusts with capital investments and tax abatements, while also proposing rule changes to make it easier for tenants to legally withhold rent in escrow.

data_points.notable_features: The plan, titled "Block by Block," is a 112-page housing strategy focused on tenant protection and landlord accountability.
data_points.project_notes: The plan proposes a $100 million publicly-backed insurance plan and explores changes to property tax calculations for majority rent-stabilized buildings.

articles.title: NBCUniversal offshoot locks down 250K sf in Midtown Manhattan
articles.narrative: Versant, an NBCUniversal spinoff, has expanded its office footprint at 229 West 43rd Street in Midtown Manhattan, adding 85,000 square feet to its existing 165,000 square feet, bringing its total to 250,000 square feet. The 18-year lease for the additional space, which includes floors 11, 12, and 14, was secured with landlord Columbia Property Trust. The property is a significant media hub, maintaining a high occupancy rate in a strong Manhattan office market.

data_points.address: 229 West 43rd Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: The space will include an outdoor terrace and custom-built studios for media brands.
data_points.project_notes: This expansion adds to 165,000 square feet taken a year earlier, bringing the total footprint to 250,000 square feet. Columbia Property Trust acquired the office portion of the building in 2015 for $516 million.

articles.market_intelligence: The Manhattan office market saw 11.8 million square feet leased in the first quarter, the strongest first quarter since 2014, with the availability rate tightening to 13.7 percent.

articles.title: South Florida’s top deals: Hillsboro Beach mansion trades for $28M
articles.narrative: An affiliate of TA Realty acquired an industrial building at 7200 Northwest 25th Street in Miami for $47.6 million. The seller, an affiliate of AEW Capital Management, had previously purchased the nearly 179,000-square-foot property in 2019 for $25 million. This transaction represents the top commercial deal in the tri-county region, with the buyer having prior ownership of the complex.

articles.title: DeSantis pushes to kill property taxes for most Floridians in special session
articles.narrative: Florida Governor Ron DeSantis is advocating for a special legislative session to consider a significant increase in the homestead exemption for primary residences, aiming to reduce property tax burdens for most Floridians. The proposal would raise the exemption from $50,000 to $250,000, eventually reaching $500,000, and would require approval from both state lawmakers and voters. DeSantis frames this as a measure to alleviate financial pressure on working residents by shifting the tax burden towards wealthier seasonal homeowners. The initiative is part of a broader trend among states exploring property tax reductions due to rising housing costs and inflation, though it faces challenges given property taxes are a major funding source for local public services.

articles.title: BH Group closes on 151-acre golf course buy after settling litigation with Straub
articles.narrative: BH Group has finalized the acquisition of the 150.5-acre Polo West Golf Course in Wellington, Palm Beach County, for $20.8 million, following the resolution of litigation with seller Glenn Straub. The Aventura-based firm plans to redevelop the shuttered golf course into a luxury single-family home community. The property, which previously hosted PGA Tour events, had been idle and subject to code citations due to Straub's unsuccessful redevelopment efforts.

data_points.notable_features: Former golf course that once hosted PGA Tour events, with previous polo fields and croquet lawns.
data_points.project_notes: Site was previously acquired by Glenn Straub from Scott Swerdlin for $16 million in 2019. Litigation between buyer and seller was settled on May 11, 2026.

articles.title: Philippe Harari’s AquaBlue under contract to sell waterfront Venetian Islands homes asking $67M
articles.narrative: AquaBlue Group has secured a buyer for its two adjacent waterfront luxury homes on the Venetian Islands in Miami Beach, with an asking price of $67 million. The properties, totaling 21,000 square feet, are expected to be completed this summer and represent the top luxury home contract signed in Miami-Dade County for the week of May 18-24. The sale highlights continued activity in the high-end residential market, with luxury homes spending an average of 168 days on the market.

data_points.address: 340 and 320 West Di Lido Drive, Miami Beach, FL
data_points.notable_features: Two adjacent waterfront homes with 120 feet of waterfront, each featuring six bedrooms, seven bathrooms, and one half-bathroom.

articles.market_intelligence: 26 luxury home contracts were signed in Miami-Dade County between May 18 and May 24, with an asking dollar volume of $333.5 million for 17 single-family homes and nine condos. Single-family homes had an average asking price of $15.6 million and spent an average of 168 days on the market. Condos had an average asking price of $7.6 million, spent 105 days on the market, and averaged $2,500 per square foot. In New York, 29 luxury home contracts were signed with a combined asking price of $228 million, spending an average of 601 days on the market.

articles.title: Coral Gables’ possible medical office buy precedes historic City Hall renovations
articles.narrative: The City of Coral Gables is considering the acquisition of the Biltmore Medical Center, a 55,000-square-foot medical office building, from Georgia-based ShareMD. This potential purchase comes as the city prepares for a $25 million to $30 million renovation of its historic City Hall, located nearby. The city is exploring using the medical office building to house various departments, including development services, parking, and human resources, to address its need for additional space during and after the City Hall renovations.

data_points.notable_features: Located in the Biltmore Way corridor, a key office hub with a mix of medical practices, law firms, accounting offices, and banks.
data_points.project_notes: The city is also undertaking a $25 million to $30 million renovation of its historic City Hall at 405 Biltmore Way, with construction starting this summer and concluding late next year. The city previously leased 10,500 square feet at 2020 Ponce for temporary staff relocation during City Hall renovations.

tenants: Asthma and Allergy Associates of Florida
Florida Center for Allergy & Asthma Care

articles.title: Chicagoland home prices in the pressure cooker
articles.narrative: Chicagoland home prices reached record highs in April, with the median sale price in the city hitting over $411,000 and the nine-county metro area reaching $390,000. This surge, significantly outpacing national trends, is driven by strong demand and limited inventory, leading to intense bidding wars and faster sales, even for luxury properties. The market is characterized by a robust seller's advantage, with many homes selling above asking price.

articles.title: DRA Advisors snaps up Plainfield apartments for $95M
articles.narrative: DRA Advisors acquired The Enclave at 127th, a 340-unit apartment complex in Plainfield, IL, from JVM Realty for $95 million. The transaction, which saw DRA Advisors assume an existing $64 million loan from Prudential Life Insurance, reflects continued investor confidence in the Chicago multifamily market. The property consists of over a dozen townhome-style buildings, each containing 20 units.

data_points.notable_features: Composed of over a dozen 20-unit townhome-style buildings.
data_points.project_notes: JVM Realty previously acquired the property in 2015 for $62 million from Continental Properties. JVM Realty refinanced the property in 2025 with a $64 million loan from Prudential Life Insurance, which DRA Advisors assumed.

articles.market_intelligence: The Chicagoland multifamily market is experiencing steady rent growth and has not seen the same value fluctuations as Sun Belt markets. Investors are increasingly drawn to the area for its stability. Nuveen affiliate acquired Uptown La Grange, a 254-unit complex, for $87.8 million in 2025. Continental Properties sold the 292-unit Springs at Weber Road complex in Romeoville for $76 million in March 2026 and Authentix McHenry, a 288-unit complex, for $55.3 million in 2025. A proposed $150 million development in St. Charles for nearly 500 units is facing challenges due to the city's inclusionary housing ordinance requiring 10% affordable units.

articles.title: Pappas says taxpayer benefits “murky” for wherever Bears land new stadium
articles.narrative: A report from the Cook County Treasurer's office casts doubt on the taxpayer benefits of proposed property tax incentives for a new Chicago Bears stadium, calling the benefits "murky." The analysis suggests that a payment in lieu of property taxes (PILOT) system, which the Bears have requested, would divert funds from schools and limit the expansion of the property tax base. The report estimates the team would receive disproportionate tax benefits compared to economic impact, despite the Bears' commitment to cover $2 billion in development costs. Governor JB Pritzker has defended the PILOT structure, emphasizing taxpayer protection in discussions.

data_points.notable_features: Proposed site for a new domed stadium and mixed-use development, formerly a race track.
data_points.project_notes: The Bears have committed $2 billion towards development costs. The proposed legislation includes a payment in lieu of property taxes (PILOT) system. The team is considering two locations: Arlington Heights, IL, or Hammond, IN.

articles.title: The Agency rides into Fort Worth
articles.narrative: The Agency, a luxury real estate brokerage based in Beverly Hills, California, has opened its ninth Texas office in Fort Worth. The new branch, located at 2833 Crockett Street, will be led by managing partner Heather Stevens and managing director Bret Whitfield. This expansion reflects a growing trend of luxury brands entering the Fort Worth market, driven by a demand for larger properties and a Western lifestyle, which some are calling the "Yellowstone effect."

data_points.address: 2833 Crockett Street No. 127, Fort Worth, TX
data_points.notable_features: Located in Fort Worth, a market experiencing increased demand for luxury properties and land.

articles.title: Plano greenlights “mini Reunion Tower” plan on future AT&T campus
articles.narrative: The Plano City Council has approved zoning for a 280-foot communication tower, designed to resemble Dallas's iconic Reunion Tower, at AT&T's new global headquarters campus in Plano. This approval is for a small portion of the 54-acre site, which will feature 2.3 million square feet of building space and parking. The telecom giant is targeting a move-in date in the second half of 2028, relocating from its current downtown Dallas lease.

data_points.notable_features: Campus will include a 280-foot communication tower designed as a 'mini Reunion Tower' replica. Buildings will be no more than eight stories.
data_points.project_notes: Plano offered a $20 million incentive package, including a 65% property tax rebate for 25 years, requiring AT&T to build amenity and retail space and occupy the building for the rebate duration. The proposed tower requires FAA approval and will not use overnight light effects. AT&T will vacate Whitacre Tower at 208 South Akard Street in Dallas, where its lease runs through 2031.

articles.title: Scott Everett’s S2 Capital faces $78M foreclosure amid REIT equity wipeout
articles.narrative: S2 Capital is facing foreclosure on The Republic Apartments in Garland, TX, after allegedly defaulting on a $78.6 million loan provided by Benefit Street Partners in 2021. The 1,033-unit multifamily property, comprising Republic West and Republic East, is scheduled for a foreclosure auction, though S2 Capital's Scott Everett stated the property is under contract for sale. This distress comes as S2 Capital's REIT, formed to manage floating-rate debt, faces significant financial challenges, with equity investors warned to expect a full loss of capital.

data_points.notable_features: Consists of two communities, Republic West and Republic East.
data_points.project_notes: S2 Capital utilized a 'traveling' housing finance corporation loophole in 2025 for tax breaks, which was later closed by House Bill 21 in May 2025.

articles.title: Paxton wins real estate industry-dividing Texas Republican primary runoff
articles.narrative: Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton secured a decisive victory over incumbent Sen. John Cornyn in the Republican primary runoff for the U.S. Senate, a race that became the most expensive in Texas history due to its perceived significance for the future direction of the Republican party. The real estate industry's endorsements were notably split, with prominent Dallas and Houston developers backing Cornyn, while other Texas-based developers supported Paxton. The outcome is seen as a bellwether for the GOP's future, attracting significant out-of-state real estate industry investment, particularly from South Florida.

articles.title: End of The Line? The Line Austin faces foreclosure after lenders took back DC and LA hotels
articles.narrative: The Line Austin hotel is facing foreclosure proceedings, with a June auction scheduled due to an alleged default on a $172 million loan originated by JP Morgan in 2023. This follows similar foreclosure actions at other Line hotel properties in Washington, D.C. and Los Angeles, where lenders took control of the assets. The Line Austin, developed by Sydell Group and opened in 2018, is a 428-key boutique hotel overlooking Congress Bridge and Lady Bird Lake.

data_points.address: 111 East Cesar Chavez Street, Austin, TX
data_points.notable_features: Overlooks Congress Bridge and Lady Bird Lake; named one of the top hotels in Texas by Condé Nast Traveler.
data_points.project_notes: The building was formerly the Crest Inn.

articles.title: Newly built affordable housing community outside Bay Area trades for $84M
articles.narrative: Hudson Valley Property Group has acquired Mosa Apartment Homes, a 387-unit affordable multifamily community in Elk Grove, for $83.6 million. The transaction involved the assumption of permanent debt and was financed through Freddie Mac loans, with LIHTC equity and public finance support. The acquisition secures affordability protections for the property for an additional 30 years, aligning with the city's housing goals.

data_points.notable_features: Garden-style property with rooftop solar panels, a fitness center, and a library. Income restrictions range from 30% to 80% of area median income.
data_points.project_notes: The property is structured across three low-income housing tax credit partnerships. Approximately 8% of units are supported by tenant-based vouchers. Affordability protections are locked in for another 30 years.

articles.financing: The acquisition included the assumption of permanent debt and was financed through three Freddie Mac loans, with LIHTC equity provided by Red Stone Equity Partners and public finance support from the California Municipal Finance Authority and the City of Elk Grove.
articles.market_intelligence: The City of Elk Grove is mandated to plan for 8,263 new housing units by 2029, with 4,265 designated as affordable.

articles.title: CA condo developers may see litigation relief as reform bill breezes through Assembly
articles.narrative: California lawmakers are advancing AB 1903, a bill aimed at reforming the state's condo defect liability law, which has been blamed for a significant decline in condo construction since 2003. The proposed legislation, which passed the State Assembly unanimously, would grant developers and general contractors a right to repair defects before facing lawsuits and offers a path to liability release with third-party quality control certification. This reform seeks to alleviate the high litigation and insurance costs that have stifled condo development, potentially boosting homeownership opportunities in a state with high housing costs.

articles.title: Touch of Bay: Former Grateful Dead studio in San Anselmo hits market
articles.narrative: A historic Marin County home, formerly a recording studio for renowned musicians, has been listed for sale at $4.4 million. The property, which includes two connected church buildings and a separate cottage, has been extensively renovated by the current owners, venture capitalist Doug Penman and his wife Samantha Lyman, since their purchase in 2014. The residence offers five bedrooms and four bathrooms across 4,300 square feet, retaining some of its original aesthetic with vaulted ceilings and exposed steel beams.

data_points.address: 1405 San Anselmo Avenue, San Anselmo, CA
data_points.notable_features: Former recording studio for artists like the Grateful Dead, Janis Joplin, and Van Morrison; includes a separate two-bedroom cottage at 21 Humboldt Avenue.
data_points.project_notes: The property was converted from a Presbyterian church into a recording studio in 1966 and later into a home. The current sellers purchased the property in 2014.

articles.market_intelligence: The median home price in Marin County is $1.5 million. A 40,000-square-foot compound in Belvedere, known as "The Crest," is currently the priciest listing in Marin County at $50 million.

articles.title: Brookfield lands Concord City Council approval for 12K-unit Navy station redevelopment
articles.narrative: Brookfield Properties has secured a crucial approval from the Concord City Council for the redevelopment of the former Concord Naval Weapons Station. The unanimous vote greenlights a financial agreement with the U.S. Navy, paving the way for a $6 billion master-planned community. This significant step follows years of stalled efforts due to labor disputes and disagreements with the Navy, and allows Brookfield to proceed with the extensive permitting and entitlement process for the multi-decade project.

data_points.notable_features: The master-planned community will include over 800 acres of parks and 25% of the residential units (3,068) will be designated as affordable housing.
data_points.project_notes: Brookfield Properties will pay the U.S. Navy $628 million over nearly 30 years for the site. The development is expected to unfold in five phases over approximately three decades, with the first phase (1,696 market-rate units and 562 affordable units) likely breaking ground in 2030.

articles.title: Quarterra plans apartment project amid rent growth in Snohomish
articles.narrative: Quarterra is planning a new 294-unit apartment complex, Emblem Snohomish, along with three commercial buildings and five townhomes at 2502 Bickford Avenue in Snohomish, Washington. The project will include 15,000 square feet of commercial space, featuring a drive-thru coffee and doughnut shop. This development aligns with strong market fundamentals in Snohomish County, which recorded the lowest multifamily vacancy rate in the Puget Sound region at 6.1% in Q1, alongside a 2% year-over-year rent increase.

data_points.notable_features: The project includes three commercial buildings and five townhomes, with a creek separating the apartment and townhome segments. One commercial building will house a 2,500-square-foot coffee and doughnut shop with a drive-thru.
data_points.project_notes: The development will replace five existing single-family homes and a shop. The properties have a combined assessed value of over $4 million, but Quarterra has not yet acquired them.

articles.market_intelligence: Snohomish County had the lowest multifamily vacancy rate in the Puget Sound region in the first quarter at 6.1%, according to Kidder Mathews data. Average rent in the county increased 2% year-over-year to $1,532.

articles.title: Uncommon Developers Takes Community-Building Approach
articles.narrative: Uncommon Developers, a vertically integrated real estate platform, focuses on community-building through its mixed-use and multifamily projects in Los Angeles. Co-founder Ryan Hekmat emphasizes job creation and strengthening local communities economically and socially by providing housing, opportunities, public spaces, and experiences that enhance quality of life. The company handles all aspects of development in-house, from acquisition to property management, and leverages modular construction to navigate market instability and rising costs. Hekmat notes that while governments aim to streamline approvals, issues like unpredictable fee schedules can undermine project viability.

data_points.notable_features: Nearly five acres dedicated to outdoor living, including dog parks, a community garden, four pools, walking paths, basketball and pickleball courts, a citrus grove, and an outdoor movie theater.
data_points.project_notes: Built on the site of a former Los Angeles Times printing facility in Chatsworth. An on-campus preschool is currently being planned.

articles.title: Lee NYC Tapped as Leasing Agent for Retail at Seward Park Cooperative
articles.narrative: Lee & Associates NYC has been appointed as the exclusive leasing agent for the retail portfolio at Seward Park Cooperative, a significant residential property in Lower Manhattan. The multi-year assignment involves managing the leasing strategy and representation for approximately 25 retail spaces totaling over 50,000 square feet. This initiative is part of a broader effort by Seward Park Cooperative to modernize and reposition its retail offerings amidst the ongoing evolution of the Lower East Side neighborhood, leveraging the area's dense residential population, strong foot traffic, and transit accessibility.

data_points.notable_features: Located in Lower Manhattan with dense residential population, strong foot traffic, and transit accessibility.

articles.title: HUD Section 223(f) Loan Refinances Chula Vista Multifamily
articles.narrative: Walker & Dunlop arranged a $101.6 million HUD Section 223(f) loan to refinance Enclave Heritage Flats, a 312-unit multifamily community in Chula Vista. This financing, secured for The Baldwin Company, replaces existing debt that Walker & Dunlop had previously arranged in 2024. The transaction highlights the HUD Section 223(f) program's appeal for high-quality multifamily assets, offering long-term, fixed-rate financing with favorable leverage and execution certainty.

data_points.address: 1802 Santa Carolina Rd, Chula Vista, CA
data_points.project_notes: Loan secured through the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s HUD Section 223(f) program. Refinances existing debt arranged by Walker & Dunlop in 2024.

companies_people.label: LENDER
companies_people.firm_name: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

articles.title: Coastal NJ Apartments and Retail Sell in 1031 Exchange
articles.narrative: Henshaw Realty acquired The Pavilion, a newly constructed 46-unit multifamily building, and an adjacent retail property in Long Branch, NJ, for a total of $19.35 million. The acquisition was part of a 1031 exchange for the buyer, while the seller, Jebara Partners, divested the assets to concentrate on core investments in Northern New Jersey. The transaction highlights continued strong investor demand for well-located multifamily assets in coastal New Jersey markets, particularly from 1031 exchange buyers seeking stable, income-producing opportunities.

data_points.notable_features: Newly constructed five-story residential building with modern finishes and private outdoor space.
data_points.project_notes: The sale included an adjacent retail property at 231 Third Ave, Long Branch, NJ, which sold for $1.35 million. The buyer utilized a 1031 exchange after selling a New York City asset.

articles.title: Blackstone, Brookfield Are Betting On Custom AI — And Proptech Could Pay The Price
articles.narrative: Wall Street titans Blackstone and Brookfield are investing heavily in custom AI solutions, signaling a shift that could disrupt the proptech sector. These firms are partnering with AI developers like Anthropic and OpenAI to create bespoke enterprise tools for real estate operations, potentially reducing the need for third-party proptech platforms. While the full impact will take time to materialize, the trend suggests a return to customized operational systems, with proptech companies needing to reposition themselves as essential data layers and systems of record to remain relevant.

articles.title: LA's Next Mayor Could Kill — Or Save — Measure ULA. Here's Where The Candidates Stand
articles.narrative: This article outlines the stances of the four leading candidates in the Los Angeles mayoral primary election on key commercial real estate issues, including housing affordability, homelessness, and the controversial Measure ULA real estate transfer tax. Incumbent Mayor Karen Bass, along with challengers Nithya Raman, Spencer Pratt, and Adam Miller, each offer distinct approaches to streamlining housing development, addressing the city's homelessness crisis, and either modifying or enforcing Measure ULA. The candidates' positions highlight the ongoing debate over how best to balance development incentives with social welfare initiatives in Los Angeles.

articles.title: Banks Fueled The Rise Of Private Credit. Now They're 'Competing Against Ourselves'
articles.narrative: Banks are facing a dilemma in the commercial real estate lending market, as their previous strategy of financing private credit funds has led to increased competition. After regulatory scrutiny and balance sheet issues constrained banks from 2023, they provided capital to debt funds, inadvertently empowering these alternative lenders. Now, as banks re-enter the market with a stronger position due to offloaded problem loans and potential policy changes, they find themselves competing against the very funds they helped capitalize. This dynamic has led banks to focus on smaller deals, largely avoiding construction lending, and continuing to act as servicers for private lenders by providing credit lines.

people.name: Tony LaBarbera
people.title: Managing Director and Co-Head of Americas Private Investments

companies_people.label: LENDER
companies_people.firm_name: BDT & MSD Partners Real Estate Credit Opportunities Fund

articles.market_intelligence: CBRE's Lending Momentum Index indicates that banks reclaimed the top spot for nonagency loan closings last year, capturing 34% of the market, though this was below historic norms. Alternative lenders have since retaken the lead, responsible for 53% of CRE debt originations in the first quarter, with debt fund deal activity surging 280%, while banks' market share fell to 22%. Committed credit lines by the largest U.S. banks to private credit vehicles increased 145% over five years, reaching $95B by Q4 2024, with $56B utilized. Most bank borrowing by private credit occurs through revolving credit lines, totaling $49B for BDCs and $30B for debt funds.

articles.title: Developers Race To Grab Spots Near TSMC's Chipmaking Factories
articles.narrative: The Phoenix real estate market is experiencing a significant boom across all asset classes, driven by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co.'s (TSMC) $165 billion investment in new chipmaking plants. Developers are actively acquiring industrial sites and land parcels, with some even demolishing existing office buildings to secure prime locations. This activity is fueled by the anticipated influx of workers and the broader ecosystem of suppliers and support services attracted to the area, creating substantial demand for industrial, multifamily, and office space. Brokers note sustained demand from direct TSMC suppliers and a wider range of users seeking proximity to the massive manufacturing project.

people.name: Josh Tracy
people.title: Senior Vice President of Real Estate Development

people.name: Hagen Hyatt
people.title: Executive Vice President of Industrial Logistics

articles.market_intelligence: Phoenix is undergoing one of the nation's most aggressive industrial build-outs. In the past year, the city reported the third-largest decline in industrial vacancy among major markets. For properties over 250K SF, vacancy dipped more than 500 basis points to below 12%. Semiconductor-adjacent land in North Phoenix is commanding unprecedented premiums due to TSMC-related demand.

articles.title: Charlotte Locals Speak Out Against Data Centers As City Moves Toward Moratorium Vote
articles.narrative: Charlotte residents are strongly advocating for a moratorium on new data center developments, citing concerns over environmental impact and resource strain. During a public hearing, citizens expressed anxieties about excessive water and power consumption by data centers, particularly in light of ongoing drought conditions and water restrictions in the city. While some argue for the economic benefits and tax revenue generated by these facilities, the overwhelming sentiment from the community is a call for a pause to reassess the long-term sustainability and planning for such projects.

data_points.notable_features: Proposed data center near Reedy Creek Nature Preserve.
data_points.project_notes: Charlotte City Council is considering a 150-day moratorium on data center development. American Tower Corp. delayed its zoning request until June 15.

articles.market_intelligence: Communities across the U.S., including the Carolinas, are experiencing backlash against data center developments, leading to an increase in moratoriums. Durham, Apex, and Chatham County have all recently passed moratoriums ranging from 60 days to one year.
